summary: Machine policy for biometric sign-in through the credential provider. Good for strict sign-in policies, but it removes fingerprint or face sign-in for local sign-in scenarios.

setting.name: Biometric Sign-in
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can let users sign in with biometrics like fingerprint or face through the credential provider. This policy controls whether that sign-in path is available.
setting.why_people_change_it: Some environments disable biometric sign-in because they want passwords, smart cards, or other methods only. Others keep it enabled because it is convenient.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling biometric sign-in removes a convenient sign-in method, but it also enforces a stricter authentication policy.
setting.professional_notes: Biometrics.admx maps the sign-in policy to SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics\Credential Provider\Enabled with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: The device does not force biometric sign-in on or off through this policy.

allowed_values.label: Biometric sign-in disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Users cannot sign in with biometrics through the credential provider.

allowed_values.label: Biometric sign-in enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Biometric sign-in is allowed through the credential provider.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes Enabled = 0 under the biometrics credential provider key, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 7 and newer systems with biometric credential provider support

states.rationale: The device policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: This policy baseline is separate from whether the device has biometric hardware enrolled.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Users who want biometric sign-in available when supported
avoid_for: Environments with a policy against biometric sign-in
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its ordinary biometric sign-in policy behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a hard policy disable for biometric sign-in.
tradeoffs: No explicit sign-in restriction is enforced.

recommended_profiles.label: No biometric sign-in
avoid_for: Users who rely on quick biometric sign-in

states.rationale: Disable biometric sign-in through the credential provider.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want passwords, smart cards, or other non-biometric methods only.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want fingerprint or face sign-in convenience to remain available.
tradeoffs: The device follows a stricter sign-in policy.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Biometrics.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file defines the biometric credential provider policy and shows Enabled with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics\Credential Provider.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes Enabled = 0 under the biometrics credential provider policy key.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Local Microsoft policy files document the exact credential provider policy surface, value semantics, and sign-in behavior. The app writes the documented disable value.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="Biometrics_EnableCredProv" class="Machine" ... key="SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics\Credential Provider" valueName="Enabled">; Biometrics.adml: If you disable this policy setting, biometrics cannot be used by any users to log on to a local Windows-based computer.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact key and value name; local ADML confirms that disabling the policy blocks biometric sign-in for local computer logon.
